channel.title: Best Mortgages For You – UK Mortgage News
channel.description: Latest UK mortgage and housing finance news, sourced and rewritten daily.

item.title: UK mortgage interest rates expected to rise despite Trump’s Iran pause
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description: ```html By Bambang Setiawan Mortgage rates expected to climb as market volatility continues UK homeowners may face shrinking mortgage options and rising interest rates in coming weeks, despite...
item.content:encoded:
```html

By Bambang Setiawan

Mortgage rates expected to climb as market volatility continues

UK homeowners may face shrinking mortgage options and rising interest rates in coming weeks, despite temporary easing in geopolitical tensions. Financial markets had initially priced in four potential Bank of England base rate increases this year, but recent developments have seen expectations moderate slightly to two quarter-point rises. However, mortgage lenders appear to be moving ahead of any official rate decisions, with average fixed rates already climbing significantly.

The average two-year fixed residential mortgage rate reached 5.43% this week - the highest level since February 2025 and up from 4.83% at the beginning of March. This upward trend comes as lenders withdraw hundreds of products from the market, with total available residential mortgage deals falling from 6,659 to 6,144 in just a few days.

Lenders react to inflation concerns

Mortgage experts suggest that lenders are proactively adjusting their offerings in anticipation of continued inflationary pressures. The recent conflict in the Middle East has created additional uncertainty in global energy markets, potentially exacerbating existing inflation concerns in the UK economy.

Nicholas Mendes of John Charcol commented: "We're seeing lenders repricing mortgages well ahead of any official Bank of England decisions. If market expectations continue pointing towards higher rates, this trend of product withdrawals and rate increases may persist."

Some analysts question whether the anticipated rate rises will materialise as predicted. The Bank of England's recent decision to maintain current rates suggests policymakers are carefully monitoring the situation, but governor Andrew Bailey has indicated that further tightening may be necessary if inflation remains stubbornly high.

What this means for borrowers

Homeowners approaching the end of their current fixed-rate deals might consider reviewing their options sooner rather than later. With lenders frequently updating their product ranges, securing a competitive rate could become increasingly challenging in the current climate.

Those considering remortgaging may wish to explore the following options:

  • Speaking with a mortgage broker about available deals
  • Considering slightly longer fixed-term products for stability
  • Reviewing equity positions as lenders adjust their criteria

While the mortgage market remains fluid, borrowers may benefit from keeping a close eye on rate movements and being prepared to act when suitable opportunities arise. As always, professional financial advice can help individuals navigate these changing conditions.

```

item.title: Why are mortgage rates going up when the Bank of England base rate hasn’t changed?
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description: By Nadya Putri Maharani Mortgage rates in the UK have been on the rise, even though the Bank of England has kept its base rate unchanged at 3.75% since December 2022. This puzzling trend has left...
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Mortgage rates in the UK have been on the rise, even though the Bank of England has kept its base rate unchanged at 3.75% since December 2022. This puzzling trend has left many homeowners questioning why borrowing costs are increasing despite a stable central bank rate. The answer lies in global economic uncertainties and how lenders manage their funding risks.

Global Events and Their Impact on Mortgage Rates

Recent geopolitical tensions, including military actions in the Middle East, have sent shockwaves through global markets. These events have led to increased volatility in stock markets, higher fuel prices, and warnings of rising costs for goods and services. Such developments influence market expectations about future interest rates, which in turn affect mortgage pricing.

Lenders often rely on wholesale markets to fund mortgages, where they borrow money at variable rates. When global uncertainty rises, lenders face increased risks, prompting them to adjust their pricing to account for potential future interest rate hikes. This is why mortgage rates can climb even when the Bank of England’s base rate remains steady.

The Role of Swap Rates in Mortgage Pricing

Swap rates play a crucial role in determining fixed-rate mortgage costs. These financial instruments allow banks to manage the risk of fluctuating interest rates. In a swap agreement, one bank agrees to pay a fixed interest rate to another bank in exchange for receiving a variable rate. This “swap” helps lenders hedge against the risk of rising interest rates.

For example, if a lender borrows £100,000 at a variable rate linked to the base rate, they might swap this into a fixed rate to ensure predictable costs. This fixed rate then forms the basis for the interest rates they offer to borrowers. When swap rates rise due to market uncertainty, lenders pass these higher costs onto homeowners in the form of increased mortgage rates.

What This Means for Borrowers

For homeowners considering a new mortgage or remortgaging, the current environment underscores the importance of staying informed about market trends. Whilst fixed-rate mortgages provide certainty, borrowers might also explore variable-rate options, which could offer better value depending on future base rate movements.

It could be worth consulting a mortgage adviser to understand how global events and swap rates might impact borrowing costs. Advisers can help identify the most suitable mortgage products based on individual circumstances and market conditions.

In summary, mortgage rates are influenced by more than just the Bank of England’s base rate. Global economic events and swap rate fluctuations play a significant role in shaping borrowing costs. By understanding these factors, homeowners can make more informed decisions about their mortgage options.

item.title: New mortgages up by £800 a year amid ‘Trumpflation’ from Iran war
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description: By Rizky Aditya Pratama Homeowners seeking new mortgage deals could face significantly higher costs, with average annual payments rising by nearly £800 in recent weeks. Market analysts attribute this...
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Homeowners seeking new mortgage deals could face significantly higher costs, with average annual payments rising by nearly £800 in recent weeks. Market analysts attribute this sharp increase to global economic instability, including geopolitical tensions in the Middle East and their impact on inflation expectations.

Mortgage rates climb amid economic uncertainty

Recent data shows the average two-year fixed mortgage rate has risen from 4.83% to 5.28% in March alone, reaching its highest level in over a year. Five-year fixed deals have followed a similar trajectory, increasing from 4.95% to 5.32%. For a typical £250,000 mortgage spread over 25 years, this translates to an extra £788 per year for a two-year fix or £651 more for a five-year deal compared to just two weeks ago.

The mortgage market has seen considerable turbulence, with lenders withdrawing nearly 700 products from the market. This represents the most significant shake-up since the 2022 mini-budget crisis. The dwindling number of competitive deals leaves borrowers with fewer options, with only nine fixed-rate mortgages currently available below 4%.

Global factors influencing UK mortgage costs

Financial experts suggest the recent rate rises stem from multiple international pressures. Rising oil and gas prices, partly driven by Middle Eastern conflicts, have renewed inflation concerns. This has led to increases in money market swap rates, which lenders use to price their fixed-rate mortgage products.

The Bank of England's anticipated interest rate cuts now appear less certain, with many analysts predicting rates will remain at 3.75% in the short term. Some commentators even suggest potential rate increases later in the year if inflationary pressures persist. These developments come at a challenging time for approximately 1.8 million homeowners whose fixed-rate deals are due to expire in 2026.

With market volatility expected to continue, borrowers might consider speaking to a mortgage adviser to understand their options. While current conditions present challenges, staying informed about rate trends and product availability could help homeowners make suitable financial decisions.

item.title: High levels of debt on essential UK bills are the ‘new normal’, warn campaigners
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description: By Sri Wahyuni Astuti Rising debt levels on essential bills have become a growing concern for many UK households, according to recent findings from the debt charity StepChange. The organisation has...
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Rising debt levels on essential bills have become a growing concern for many UK households, according to recent findings from the debt charity StepChange. The organisation has highlighted that increasing arrears on housing, energy, and council tax payments have become the “new normal” for low-income families. With budgets stretched by persistent cost pressures, many are struggling to keep up with everyday expenses, despite some easing in energy prices and slower growth in mortgage and rental costs.

Growing Arrears Across Essential Payments

StepChange’s data reveals a worrying trend, with average rent arrears rising by 15% to £2,372, while mortgage arrears saw a significant 22% increase, climbing from £10,239 in 2024 to £12,534 in 2025. Energy debt also remains a pressing issue, despite reductions in prices since their peak in 2022. Over a third of the charity’s clients were in debt to energy companies, with the average debt increasing by £220 to £2,560. Although this figure is slightly lower than the previous year, it underscores the ongoing financial strain faced by many households.

The charity’s analysis also shows that two in five clients were receiving universal credit, while three in five lived in rented accommodation. This highlights the vulnerability of those on low incomes or reliant on benefits, who are disproportionately affected by rising costs.

Calls for Government Intervention

StepChange has urged the government to take further action to support households struggling with essential bills. The charity has proposed the introduction of national social tariffs for energy and water, which could help reduce costs to a more manageable level for those on low incomes or with high needs. Vikki Brownridge, Chief Executive of StepChange, emphasised that the cost of everyday essentials remains prohibitively high for many families, with rising arrears showing little sign of slowing down.

Brownridge stated, “The reality is that rising essential bills and arrears across housing, energy, and consumer credit debt have become the new normal for many households. Our client data has consistently reflected this pressure over several years, and without intervention, the situation is unlikely to improve.”

As households continue to face financial challenges, addressing the root causes of rising debt and ensuring access to affordable essentials will be crucial in helping families regain stability.

item.title: UK housing costs rise 41% over five years for renters and owners, study shows
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description: By Bambang Setiawan New research reveals that UK housing costs have surged by 41% over the past five years, reaching a record £226bn in 2023. This sharp increase has impacted both homeowners and...
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

New research reveals that UK housing costs have surged by 41% over the past five years, reaching a record £226bn in 2023. This sharp increase has impacted both homeowners and renters, with mortgage holders facing particularly steep rises in payments. According to property group Savills, the total spent on housing has climbed by £66bn since 2018, with mortgage interest payments accounting for more than half of this increase.

Mortgage Holders Bear the Brunt

Mortgage borrowers have been hit hard by rising interest rates, with payments growing by 9% last year alone. Savills estimates that 8.8 million mortgage holders paid a combined £114bn in 2023, equating to an average of £13,000 per household annually. This spike is partly due to homeowners rolling off fixed-rate deals and facing higher borrowing costs in a challenging economic climate.

Recent global events, including geopolitical tensions, have raised concerns about persistent inflation, which could keep mortgage rates elevated. Lucian Cook, Savills’ head of residential research, noted that the impact of higher rates on household finances may linger longer than expected. “With homeowners fixing their mortgages for longer periods, the effects of increased interest rates could continue to weigh on spending power,” he explained.

Rental Costs on the Rise

While renters have seen slower cost increases compared to homeowners, private rental bills have still climbed by 27% over the past five years. In 2023, UK renters paid a total of £112bn, with private sector landlords receiving £81bn of this amount – an average of £15,000 per landlord. Despite the slower pace of growth, rental affordability remains a pressing issue for many tenants.

Regional Variations

Housing cost increases have varied significantly across the UK. London recorded the smallest percentage rise at 36%, although it still accounts for the largest share of the nation’s housing costs at 23.4%. In contrast, the north-west saw the steepest increase at 49%, followed by the north-east and eastern England, both at 45%. These regional disparities highlight the uneven impact of rising housing costs across the country.

With mortgage rates continuing to fluctuate and global economic uncertainty persisting, homeowners and renters alike may wish to carefully review their financial planning. Staying informed about market trends and exploring available options could help mitigate the impact of rising housing costs.
